petitioner_background: The petitioner is an athletic shoe store affiliated with New Balance, seeking EB-1A classification for the beneficiary, a Kenyan former elite runner who won the gold medal in the 1,500 meters at the 1988 Seoul Olympics. After retiring from active sports, the beneficiary established the Educational and Training Center (PRETC) in Kapsabet, Kenya, which identifies prospective student athletes for U.S. colleges, and has been working for the petitioner as a coach and consultant in O-1 nonimmigrant status since January 2001. He coaches runners and racewalkers sponsored by the shoe store, gives motivational speeches, and recruits Kenyan athletes for U.S. universities.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Photocopy of the beneficiary's Olympic gold medal from the 1,500 meters race at the 1988 Seoul Olympics; references to several other international and national running competitions won by the beneficiary (without primary evidence); a copy of a plaque reading 'NCAA II Cross Country Hall of Fame, 2003'; petitioner's statement that admission to the NCAA Hall of Fame is 'a prestigious and select body, which includes character as an important qualification.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO treated the Olympic gold medal separately as a potential one-time achievement, finding that while Olympic gold medals demonstrate an athlete has reached the top of the field, they may only establish sustained acclaim if the athlete remains active — and here the beneficiary had retired. For lesser prizes, the record contained no primary evidence of the other claimed awards or of prizes won by athletes the beneficiary coached (which could have served as comparable evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4)). The record contained no independent evidence supporting the claim about or documenting the significance of the NCAA Hall of Fame award.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Excerpts from two books and two trade publications documenting the beneficiary's 1988 Olympic gold medal performance; an article entitled 'Kenya's Olympics' from the November 1988 New African containing a drawing and photograph of the beneficiary; a publication of Mount Saint Mary's College entitled 'Mount Track '88-'89' featuring photographs of the beneficiary at the Olympics and a short article listing his running accomplishments; on appeal, an article entitled 'World Marathon Record Holder Visits Lyndhurst' published in the June 10, 2004 edition of The Commercial Leader about the beneficiary's role in arranging a visit of Kenyan marathoner to New Jersey.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence that the books and periodicals constitute professional or major trade publications or other major media. All the articles relate to the beneficiary's athletic achievements as a runner, with no published material concerning his work as a coach or consultant — the claimed field of expertise. The articles do not reflect sustained acclaim in the beneficiary's claimed field. The Commercial Leader article was disregarded because it was published six months after the petition was filed, and eligibility must be established at the time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Claims that the beneficiary was selected to coach athletes from Africa at the IAAF-sponsored training camp and was an assistant coach in track and field at Mount Saint Mary's College; multiple recommendation letters indicating the beneficiary judges work of others as a recruiter of Kenyan athletes for U.S. colleges and universities, including letters from the IAAF communications director (stating the beneficiary is 'universally respected' for his work on behalf of young student-athletes), letters confirming he recruited or assisted more than 200 Kenyan students for scholarships in American universities, a letter from a second-place NCAA finisher confirming he was recruited by the beneficiary, a letter from sports journalist John Manners stating 'dozens of young men and women' recruited and developed by the beneficiary have achieved distinction including a World Indoor Championship medalist, a letter from a professor of Coaching Education at Kennesaw State University, and a letter from the Track and Cross Country Coach at Central Arizona College stating the beneficiary helped recruit eight student-athletes leading to two National Championships in track and field.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO acknowledged coaching may establish eligibility when reflecting sustained acclaim, but found insufficient evidence of the beneficiary's work as a coach. The purported IAAF coaching selection and Mount Saint Mary's position might satisfy the criterion if adequately documented, but the record contained no primary evidence of either position or documentation that selection reflected sustained national or international acclaim. Regarding recruiting, although arguably within the claimed field, the record contained no corroborative documentation of his work as a recruiter or the accomplishments of recruited students beyond one student. There was insufficient evidence that his recruiter role gained recognition in the field beyond individuals with whom he worked directly or who had personal knowledge of his accomplishments.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the beneficiary's valuable contributions to the cultivation of young athletes in Kenya and their recruitment by U.S. colleges; two articles written by the beneficiary urging reform of the Kenya Amateur Athletics Association (KAAA); recommendation letters on appeal praising charitable endeavors including a letter from a current World Record holder in the marathon who stated the beneficiary inspired him to become involved in activities benefiting the world beyond athletics, leading to his appointment as a UN World Food Programme Ambassador against Hunger.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no documentation of the impact of the reform articles on their intended audience. While charitable work was acknowledged as commendable, the record contained insufficient documentation of the beneficiary's exact activities and the impact of this work in his field. The evidence did not demonstrate that the charitable work was unique or original among former athletes, or that it significantly influenced other track and field coaches and consultants.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Copies of two articles written by the beneficiary urging reform of the KAAA, published in the January 5, 2002 edition of the Saturday Nation, which appears to be a Kenyan newspaper.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record contained no circulation or publication information about the Saturday Nation or any other evidence establishing that the newspaper is a professional publication, major trade publication, or nationally circulated newspaper in Kenya.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence that the beneficiary works as a coach and consultant for the petitioner's New Balance-affiliated shoe store; petitioner's statements crediting the beneficiary with putting the store 'on the map' and making it the number 1 or 2 ranked New Balance store in the country for the last three years and assembling a collection of national champion athletes; the beneficiary's statement that he established the Educational and Training Center (PRETC) in Kapsabet, Kenya.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no organizational chart or other documentation establishing the beneficiary's exact position at the store, though he is clearly a highly valued employee. The petitioner provided no corroborative documentation supporting claims crediting the beneficiary with the store's success, and simply going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient to meet the burden of proof, citing Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158, 165 (Comm. 1998) and Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1973). The record was devoid of independent evidence that the shoe store has a distinguished reputation. Regarding PRETC, there was no evidence the beneficiary performed in a leading or critical role or of PRETC's reputation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Form I-140 indicating the beneficiary would be paid $962 per week; on appeal, counsel's claim that the beneficiary is 'highly paid' for a franchised reseller of a major athletic company; a letter explaining that 'the overall decline in media attention given to track, particularly in the U.S.' has led to a corresponding decline in the market value of the sport's stars, and opining that because the beneficiary is from Kenya with so many brilliant runners, his ability to command a high salary is further limited; petitioner's statement that they 'aren't a highly capitalized entity.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded the salary 'seems minimal for an individual of extraordinary ability caliber' and noted the record contained no documentation of present or past income. The AAO noted that if statements about the limited economic clout of top track and field athletes were true, the beneficiary would be better served by not claiming eligibility under this criterion. However, because counsel claimed he is 'highly paid,' the AAO was obliged to discuss eligibility. The record contained no documentation of income or other evidence to support counsel's claim.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO determined that while the beneficiary won international acclaim as an Olympic gold medalist in 1988, he has since retired from active sports and now works as a coach and consultant for a shoe store while engaging in charitable activities. The record did not sufficiently demonstrate that the beneficiary's past acclaim as an Olympic medalist has been sustained through his subsequent work as a coach or consultant. Although the nexus between competing and coaching allows adjudicators to consider the totality of evidence when an athlete has sustained acclaim in coaching at a national level, the record contained insufficient evidence to document the beneficiary's claimed coaching work, let alone establish sustained national or international acclaim for his coaching. The petitioner failed to meet the burden of proof under Section 291 of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1973)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted a procedural timing issue: the article 'World Marathon Record Holder Visits Lyndhurst' from The Commercial Leader submitted on appeal was published six months after the petition was filed and could not be considered, as the beneficiary's eligibility must be established at the time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
